perturbation.original_text: h. "Enterprise" means any legal entity and the subsidiaries it owns by more than 50 percent. The term "Enterprise" applies only to the portion of the Enterprise physically located within the United States of America.
perturbation.changed_text: h. "Enterprise" means any legal entity and the subsidiaries it owns by more than 50 percent, regardless of the physical location of the enterprise or its subsidiaries.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the geographical limitation stating that the term 'Enterprise' applies only to the portion of the Enterprise physically located within the United States of America, the definition is broadened, which has implications for liability, data protection, and other clauses tied to this definition. This can create uncertainty in enforcement, particularly concerning subsidiaries located outside the U.S.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of corporate law and jurisdictional limits
perturbation.law_citation: 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts general principles of corporate law and jurisdictional limits by implying that a U.S. court can exercise jurisdiction over an 'Enterprise' (and its subsidiaries) located entirely outside the U.S., simply because the parent company has a contract governed by U.S. law. 28 U.S. Code § 1332 establishes requirements for diversity jurisdiction, which is affected by the location of the parties. This section would be important to determine if a case involving the 'Enterprise' could be heard in federal court.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between— (1) citizens of different States; (2) citizens of a State and citizens or subjects of a foreign state, except that the district courts shall not have original jurisdiction under this subsection of an action between citizens of a State and citizens or subjects of a foreign state who are lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States and are domiciled in the same State; (3) citizens of different States and in which citizens or subjects of a foreign state are additional parties; and (4) a foreign state, defined in section 1603(a) of this title , as plaintiff and citizens of a State or of different States. (b) Except when express provision therefor is otherwise made in a statute of the United States, where the plaintiff who files the case originally in the Federal courts is finally adjudged to be entitled to recover less than the sum or value of $75,000, computed without regard to any setoff or counterclaim to which the defendant may be adjudged to be entitled, and exclusive of interest and costs, the district court may deny costs to the plaintiff and, in addition, may impose costs on the plaintiff. (c) For the purposes of this section and section 1441 of this title — (1) a corporation shall be deemed to be a citizen of every State and foreign state by which it has been incorporated and of the State or foreign state where it has its principal place of business, except that in any direct action against the insurer of a policy or contract of liability insura
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the geographical limitation on the definition of 'Enterprise,' which originally confined the term to entities physically located within the U.S. The Law Explanation suggests this contradicts jurisdictional limits, potentially implying U.S. courts could exert authority over enterprises entirely outside the U.S. Snippet 2, referencing 28 U.S. Code § 1332, pertains to diversity of citizenship and jurisdiction. By broadening the definition of 'Enterprise' to include entities regardless of location, the modified text could affect the determination of jurisdiction, as the location of the parties is relevant under 28 U.S.C. 1332, thus creating a contradiction. The removal of the geographical limitation directly opposes the initial intent to limit the enterprise to the USA and is a significant change, warranting a YES.

perturbation.original_text: 3.3 Termination for Cause Customer or IBM may terminate this Agreement for material breach of this Agreement by the other upon written notice containing the specific nature and dates of the material breach. The breaching party will have thirty (30) days from receipt of notice to cure such breach, except for nonpayment by Customer, which must be cured within seven (7) business days from receipt of notice.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.3 Termination for Cause Customer or IBM may terminate this Agreement for any breach of this Agreement by the other upon written notice. The breaching party will have thirty (30) days from receipt of notice to cure such breach, including nonpayment by Customer.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "material breach" to "any breach" significantly expands the grounds for termination, while removing the accelerated cure period for non-payment. This contradicts standard contract law principles that differentiate between material and minor breaches and creates uncertainty in enforcement, as any trivial breach could lead to termination.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law Principles of Contract Law - Material Breach Doctrine
perturbation.law_citation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 241 - Circumstances Significant in Determining Whether a Failure Is Material
perturbation.law_explanation: The 'Material Breach Doctrine', found in Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 241, states that only a material breach allows the non-breaching party to suspend performance or terminate the contract. The altered text allows termination for any breach, contradicting this established principle. The Cornell Law link provides a more general definition of Contract Law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies termination for 'material breach,' aligning with standard contract law. The changed text broadens this to 'any breach,' which contradicts the established legal principle that not every breach justifies termination. The Wex Law snippet from LII defines a contract as an agreement creating mutual obligations enforceable by law, and notes that if a promise is breached, the law provides remedies. By allowing termination for 'any breach', even minor ones, the modified contract deviates from this principle of proportionate remedies and undermines the stability of the contractual agreement, as any trivial infraction could lead to termination.

perturbation.original_text: 4.2 Payment IBM invoices will specify the amount due. Payment is due upon receipt and payable as specified in such invoice. Customer agrees to pay accordingly, including any late payment fees. Payment will be made in United States dollars.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2 Payment IBM invoices will specify the amount due. Payment is due 90 days after receipt, irrespective of what is on the invoice. Customer agrees to pay accordingly, including any late payment fees. Payment will be made in a mutually agreed upon currency.
perturbation.explanation: The changed payment terms contradicts the original agreement, by saying that invoices are due 90 days after receipt and agreeing on a mutually agreed upon currency. This would introduce a major conflict, since payments are due immediately upon reciept, and should be paid in United States dollars.
perturbation.law_citation: UCC § 2-310 - Open Time for Payment or Running of Credit; Authority to Ship Under Reservation
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-310, which governs the sale of goods, generally states that payment is due at the time and place at which the buyer is to receive the goods unless otherwise agreed. While parties can contract around this, standard commercial practice and legal expectations support prompt payment. The modified text introduces a lengthy 90-day payment term, which creates potential issues related to cash flow and the seller's ability to operate effectively, and goes against generally accepted accounting principles.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-310. Open Time for Payment or Running of Credit; Authority to Ship Under Reservation. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-310. Open Time for Payment or Running of Credit; Authority to Ship Under Reservation. Unless otherwise agreed (a) payment is due at the time and place at which the buyer is to receive the goods even though the place of shipment is the place of delivery; and (b) if the seller is authorized to send the goods he may ship them under reservation, and may tender the documents of title, but the buyer may inspect the goods after their arrival before payment is due unless such inspection is inconsistent with the terms of the contract (Section 2-513 ); and (c) if delivery is authorized and made by way of documents of title otherwise than by subsection (b) then payment is due at the time and place at which the buyer is to receive the documents regardless of where the goods are to be received; and (d) where the seller is required or authorized to ship the goods on credit the credit period runs from the time of shipment but post-dating the invoice or delaying its dispatch will correspondingly delay the starting of the credit period . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts standard commercial practice by introducing a 90-day payment term and allowing for payment in a mutually agreed upon currency, as opposed to US dollars. UCC § 2-310 states that "payment is due at the time and place at which the buyer is to receive the goods unless otherwise agreed". While 'unless otherwise agreed' allows for deviation, the original contract clearly stated payments were due upon receipt in US dollars, and the change directly contradicts this established agreement. The new terms significantly alter the payment conditions, creating a legal contradiction with the original agreement and the expectation of prompt payment.
